content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Colleen Winchester 
Assistant City Attorney 
City of Cupertino 
20410 Town Center Lane, Suite 210 
Cupertino, CA 95014-3255 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-023 
Dear Ms. Winchester: 
March 3, 2016 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of City Council member Rod Sinks 
regarding the gift provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1 
QUESTION 
Are payments for Councilmember Sinks' travel related expenses paid by two 501(c)(3) non­
profit organizations for speaking al two separate conferences reportable gifts; and are such 
payments subject to the limitations for honoraria or gifts? 
CONCLUSION 
Payments for travel, lodging and subsistence which are reasonably related lo a government 
purpose and provided by a 501 (c)(3) non-profit organizations are reportable gifts, but are not 
subject to gift limits. However, if Councilmember Sinks accepts gifts from either non-profit entity of 
$460 or more, he may be prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting those 
sources. 
FACTS 
Councilmember Sinks was invited to speak at two conferences involving issues of policy for 
state and local governments. 
First, Councilmember Sinks was invited to speak at the "New Partners for Smart Growth 
Conference" in Portland, Oregon. The Conference occurred on February 11-13, 2016.2 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 181 10 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatorr references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 
- The Commission will not advise on conducl that has already occurred. Because your request for advice was 
received on February 3, 2016, five business days before the official was to receive travel, we provide this advice 


File No. A-16-023 
Page No. 2 
Councilmember Sinks was invited to speak in the session: "Mayors' Challenge for Safer People, 
Safer Streets," as one of three mayors discussing the progress their cities have made in advancing 
bicycle and pedestrian safety, and accessibility. The Institute for Local Government, Leaders for 
Livable Communities ("ILG"), a 501 (c)(3) non-profit organization, has offered to pay for one hotel 
night, plane flight, and one day of conference-related expenses. 
Second, Councilmember Sinks was invited to speak at the "Rise of Smart Cities Conference 
on Information and Communications and Technology: Getting from Vision to Reality 
for Broadband-Enabled Cities," in Sonoma, California. The conference is scheduled for March 3 -
4, 2016. Councilmember Sinks will be speaking about implementing a Smart Growth City vision, 
with a major broadband upgrade. The California Foundation on the Environment and the Economy 
("CFEE"), a 50 l (c)(3) non-profit organization, has offered to pay one hotel night and group meal 
expenses. 
ANALYSIS 
Gifts: Limitatio11s, Reporti11g, Co11flicts of lllterest 
Generally, a gift is defined under Section 82028(a) as "any payment that confers a personal 
benefit on the recipient, to the extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and 
includes a rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is made 
in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to official status." In an 
effort to reduce improper influences on public officials, the Act regulates the receipt of gifts by 
local public officials in three ways: 
• 
First, the Act places limitations on the acceptance of gifts by certain public officials. The 
current limit is $460 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section 89503.) 
• Second, so that the public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts, the Act 
imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gift of $50 or 
more (or gifts that aggregate to $50 or more from the same source) received during the 
calendar year are disclosed on the officials' statement<; of economic interests. (Sections 
87200-87314.) 
• Third, the Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence the outcome of a governmental decision involving the donor 
of a gift or gifts with an aggregate value of $460 or more provided to, received by, or 
promised to the official within the 12 months prior to the date the decision is made. 
(Sections 87100 & 87103(e).) 
Gifts of Travel Expe11ses 
Under the Act, travel is presumed to confer some personal benefit on a public official. 
Consequently, travel costs paid for by a third party generally are reportable gifts under the Act. 
prospectively in light of the return rule in Section 82028(b )(2). This rule provides, if no exception applies, that the term 
"gift" docs not include payments not used or returned within 30 days of receipt under the Act. 


File No. A-16-023 
Page No. 3 
Therefore, absent an exception, the value of all travel-related expenses wilJ be considered a 
reportable gift. (Section 82028.) 
Gifts of Travel Expenses whell a speech is givell; Honorarium 
Generally speaking, under the Act, "honorarium" includes "any payment made in 
consideration for any speech given, article published or attendance at a conference." (Section 
89501 (a).) However, pursuant to Section 89501 ( c ), all travel payments are governed by Section 
89506 and are not considered "honorarium." In this matter, Councilmember Sinks is being offered 
travel payments only, based the facts provided, and Section 89506 will apply.3 
Gifts of Travel, Exception to the Gift Limit 
Section 89506 provides that under some circumstances, even though a payment for 
transportation, lodging, and subsistence may be a reportable gift under the Act, the gift is not 
subject to the Act's current $460 gift limit. Additionally, travel payments may be allowed in 
connection with a speech by a public official. Section 89506(a) provides in pertinent part: 
(a) Payments, advances, or reimbursements, for travel, including actual 
transportation and related lodging and subsistence that is reasonably related to a 
legislative or governmental purpose, or to an issue of.state, national, or 
international public policy, are not prohibited or limited by this chapter if either 
of the followi11g apply: 
( J) The travel is in connection with a speech given by the elected state officer, 
local elected officeholder, candidate for elective state office or local elective 
office, . .. the lodging and subsistence expenses are limited to the day 
immediately preceding, the day of, and the day immediately following the speech, 
and the travel is within the United States. 
(2) The travel is provided by a government, a governmental agency, a foreign 
government, a governmental authority, a bona fide public or private educational 
institution, as defined in Section 203 of the Revenue and Taxation Code, a 
nonprofit charitable or religious organization which is exempt from taxation under 
Section 501 (c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code, or by a person domiciled outside 
the United States which substantially satisfies the requirements for tax-exempt 
status under Section 50l(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. (emphasis added) 
According to the facts, CounciJmember Sinks' participation in each conference relates to 
issues of policy for state and local governments and the governmental purpose of effective 
government. The travel is in connection with a speech, the travel expenses will be limited to travel 
in the United States, and the lodging and subsistence expenses limited to the day preceding, or the 
3 Please note: non-travel payment or items received in consideration for giving a speech arc considered to be 
"honorarium." (Section 89501.) Public officials are banned from accepting such ilems, with certain particular 
exceptions. (Sections 8950 I (b) and 89502; see also Regulations 18930, ct seq., for exceptions and special rules.) We 
have no facts lo suggest that an honorarium is at issue for either conference appearance. 


File No. A-16-023 
Page No. 4 
day of the speech. Moreover, the travel expenses in each instance will be paid by a 50l(c)(3) 
nonprofit organization. 
Accordingly, the specified payments are reportable gifts not subject to gift limits under Section 
89506(a)( I) and (2). 
Please note, however, that any payments made for items other than travel and related lodging 
and subsistence may be considered a reportable gift subject to the $460 gift limit and the conflict of 
interest provisions of the Act.4 In addition, if either of the non-profit entities were merely an 
intermediary for other donors, the gifts would be attributed to the true source, and Section 89506 
may not apply. (See Regulation I 8945(a).) We have no facts to suggest that either non-profit entity 
is acting as an intermediary for the payments or that they are provided by other sources. 
Conflict of /11terest Disquali.ficatio11 
A public official who receives gift(s) of $460 or more may have a financial conflict of interest 
under the Act. (See Regulations 18700 and 18941.) The official must disqualify himself or herself from 
voting or otherwise participating in a governmental decision affecting that source, if the payment was 
received or promised to the official within 12 months preceding the decision. Therefore, if 
Councilmernber Sinks accept gifts from either non-profit entity valued at $460 or more, he may be 
prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting those sources. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division 
c Payments made for items other than travel and related lodging and subsistence, such as expenses paid for 
entertainment purposes will be considered reportable gifts subject to the $460 gift limit because those items would not 
be related to a legislative or governmental purpose. We have no facts to suggest the offer to cover travel expenses 
includes personal charges, such as gifts and activities not part of the formal program.

$460 or more, he may be prohibited from participating in governmental decisions affecting those 
sources.

CONCLUSION: Payments for travel, lodging and subsistence which are reasonably related lo a government

subject to gift limits. However, if Councilmember Sinks accepts gifts from either non-profit entity of
